Way to mentally connect this. And so, when you think about, so Darren's business is selling, investing in Iowa real estate to people who don't live in Iowa. So, if you think of what would appear to be an easy task, would be say selling, investing in, forget the current market, investing in sexy high rise condos in South Beach Miami to people who don't live there, or Las Vegas, or maybe even Costa Rica. But Iowa is not sort of inherently, you know, it doesn't seem like that would be an easy thing to do. But it is then all about creating and selling place, in most cases to people who have come from that place, who still have some emotional connection to small town, main street, shops, flags on porches on 4th of July, you know, almost a Disney-fied small town America that may not even exist there exactly anymore, but is romanced in their own mind that they want to reconnect to and be a part of. If you go to some of you guys who live there, undoubtedly driven through celebration in Orlando, Disney has created that to actually sell to people to live in rather than to invest in. And it is like the Andy Griffith show, you know, delivered perfectly in real life. I mean, I don't think they have fake people walking around there anywhere, but I'm not sure, it's like that close. I like the place, Carla felt it was a little stepford-wivey for her, it like bothered her that it was like so perfect. It's almost like you're in a play, you know, and, but of course they're selling this stuff as fast as they can build it. And so, see, I think for you, and when you say you don't do a very good job with it now, what did you mean? Tell them a story, tell them a specific story, why, you know, why here versus any other alternative, and not really connecting with them through that. I mean, I may be devote paragraph, maybe half a paragraph to it, and that's it. I need to devote, and that's maybe in one piece of marketing material they get. So, I mean, that needs to be in everything. And we know, and we know from studying your client base, that there's a substantial majority of these people who are root connected to this place, which means they're emotionally connected to this place. And yeah, I agree, see, I think it's 50% of everything you ever say to them is, and so, like your newsletter should be less fact figure real estate stuff and more about what's going on at the Iowa State Fair, and who had the biggest pumpkin this year, and boy, it's a shame you weren't here, and, you know, because really you're selling values. That's what you're selling, a profound value connection. And you have a natural place, part of your business, to transport to them. It would be nice for them to have a newsletter that has a cracker barrel kind of theme to it, and it's a lot about Iowa and a little bit about real estate. There's never a photograph of anything, like whatever the holidays and Cedar Rapids. Instead of the photograph, there's like a pence and sketch, and here's the little, the Snopo 76 station where you can still get your gas pump through wherever it goes. See, now you're into visuals, symbolism, things we talked about before, and so, you know, you essentially have an easy time of it. But we all need to think about this in terms of how they think about us and what they're a part of. Okay, 107, principle of high concept. You want an umbrella idea under which you operate that can have some legs for some time that is easy. They get it, easy to understand, and gives you a lot of room underneath it. It encompasses sort of traditional thought about brand, but it's really more than brand. So that it has meaning to them when heard and seen. In some cases, it has brand value in the sense that they want it on things. It's the idea of being able to do something like MTV cops and we wind up in Miami Vice. Okay, I get it. It's that concise, it's that clever, and it's that telegraphic, preferably benefit-based, but not always. So zero resistance is benefit-based. Renegade Millionaire is not really benefit-based. It leaves some interpretation room, but everybody has a thought about it when they hear it. And it's aspirational in the sense that the greatest conformists among us like to think of themselves as unconformists. Everybody likes the idea of being a renegade even though they may not behave in that manner. The recently held secret high concept stolen from Earl Nightingale going back to lead the field. And so great businesses of great influence have a great high concept. Disney's Happiest Place on Earth is a fabulous high concept, so it makes it much more than a place of rides and attractions. It's something that like Universal can't, it's Six Flags, can't figure out why they get their butt kicked. It's because they're still behaving as if they're in the amusement park business and Disney behaves as if they're in a very, very different business. And so you have to think about what umbrella you have over everything that you are doing. 108-111 talks about that some. I'll let you read that at your own pace on your own time. So let's go to 112 and we're going to very quickly start to get to and kind of finish the day with something pretty important, which is building the profile of the person we intend to influence. So principle 16 is desires met. And so this is to influence people, give them what they want. And it is interesting in a sense how much time a lot of people spend trying to sell people things that they don't want. And then, you know, wonder why they're having such a tough time. And so the best engineering process is whether they want, then how can we fulfill that within the parameters of what we're willing to fulfill and what we're willing to, you know, deliver. And primarily above all of this, what people want is simple certainties. They don't want complex answers to complex problems. They want simple answers to complex problems. And they want them to be very certain and very definitive. So here's two examples. Like we just had on, I guess, yesterday's Go Plus call. So one of the however, how many things were on the list, eight? Okay, so the first thing on the list of the eight had to do with mailing lists. And whenever I have the mailing list conversation, if I am physically present with people when I have it, I can see their eyes glaze over and the restlessness start. Because really what everybody wants to be told is, what list should I mail to and where should I get it? They don't like this answer of finding list biases and reverse engineering the list and merge purging. And there's only a segment of a list that's going to be successful for you, not the whole list. See, that's a very complex answer to what most people view as a simple question. Now I have never found a way to give them a simple answer to it. Consequently, by the way, I try not to talk about it with the uninitiated at all. Because giving people answers they don't want early is even, you know, a bigger problem than giving it to them late. If you contrast that with, say, what Maltz did with psychocybernetics. So, you know, and there's a book, see, when you look at these classics like, Think and Grow Rich and Psycho-Cybernetics, and Psycho-Cybernetics has been in print nonstop since 1960. It sells at least 100,000 copies every year.
